content:
3 min read
14 hours ago

--

I’ve been working on OOP AI, an experimental personal project that explores a different way of providing context to AI coding assistants.
Instead of feeding the model a large collection of project rules, OOP AI organizes instructions as an object-oriented context hierarchy, where each task only loads the contexts it actually needs. The goal is simple: keep AI context focused, reusable, and easier to maintain while reducing unnecessary information during development.
Recently, I decided to compare this approach with GitHub’s Spec Kit, one of the most interesting structured AI development workflows available today.
The Experiment
To make the comparison as fair as possible, I created a clean Angular 17 project and prepared two separate branches:

• Branch 1: OOP AI setup

• Branch 2: GitHub Spec Kit setup
Both branches started from exactly the same project.
The development task was intentionally simple:

• Create a login page

• Implement a mock authentication service

• Redirect authenticated users to an empty dashboard page
The idea wasn’t to stress-test the frameworks with complexity, but rather to compare how each workflow guides an AI assistant through a real feature implementation.
OOP AI Workflow
For OOP AI I used the Generate Feature Context workflow.
The prompt was very straightforward:
Generate a login page with an input component for email and password, a button component, a mock login service, and a dashboard page.
The workflow first generated the required context files (feature contexts), and then those contexts were used during implementation.
The entire development was performed using OpenCode.
Results

• ⏱️ 10 minutes

• 🧠 41,000 tokens

• ✅ Complete implementation
GitHub Spec Kit Workflow
For the second branch I configured GitHub Spec Kit and used its standard workflow:

• /speckit.specify

• /speckit.plan

• /speckit.tasks

• /speckit.implement
I provided a prompt equivalent to the one used with OOP AI, keeping the requested feature identical.
Again, everything was executed using OpenCode.
Results

• ⏱️ 25 minutes

• 🧠 30,000 tokens

• ✅ Complete implementation
Spec Kit spent more time creating specifications and planning before implementation, but ultimately consumed fewer tokens than OOP AI.
What’s Next?
OOP AI is still a personal experimental project, and one of my current priorities is reducing its token consumption without sacrificing the speed and modularity that its context inheritance model provides.
I’m continuously iterating on the architecture and experimenting with different context-loading strategies to make future versions more efficient.
If you’re interested in AI-assisted software development, context engineering, or alternative workflows for coding assistants, I’d love to hear your thoughts and feedback.
